questions.question: Explain Modus Ponens and Modus Tollens with examples.
keyPoints: Define Modus Ponens: (P→Q)∧P ⇒ Q with example like 'If it rains, ground is wet. It rained. Therefore ground is wet.'
Define Modus Tollens: (P→Q)∧¬Q ⇒ ¬P with example like 'If it rains, ground is wet. Ground is not wet. Therefore it did not rain.'
Note both are valid inference rules

questions.question: What is the difference between a valid argument and a sound argument?
keyPoints: Valid: structure ensures truth of conclusion whenever premises are true
Sound: valid argument + all premises are actually true in real world
A sound argument guarantees a true conclusion
An unsound argument may have false conclusion despite valid structure

questions.question: List and explain any four inference rules in propositional logic.
keyPoints: Modus Ponens, Modus Tollens, Hypothetical Syllogism, Disjunctive Syllogism, Addition, Simplification, Conjunction (any four)
Give formal notation for each
Explain with examples

questions.question: What are the common fallacies in reasoning? Explain with examples.
keyPoints: Affirming the consequent: P→Q, Q therefore P (wrong)
Denying the antecedent: P→Q, ¬P therefore ¬Q (wrong)
Give clear examples of each fallacy
Explain why these are invalid

questions.question: Explain the Resolution principle in propositional logic.
keyPoints: Resolution: (P∨Q) ∧ (¬P∨R) ⇒ Q∨R
Used in automated theorem proving
Explain with example
Only resolves complementary literals

questions.question: What is Constructive Dilemma? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: Formal: (P→Q) ∧ (R→S) ∧ (P∨R) ⇒ Q∨S
Both implications true, disjunction of antecedents leads to disjunction of consequents
Example with real-world scenario
Compare with Destructive Dilemma

questions.question: Using truth table method, determine if the argument: P→Q, Q→R ⊢ P→R is valid.
keyPoints: Construct truth table with P, Q, R columns
Identify rows where both premises P→Q and Q→R are true
In those rows, check if conclusion P→R is also true
Show all 8 combinations and verify validity

questions.question: Explain the natural deduction system for propositional logic. Discuss how to prove validity using various inference rules with examples.
keyPoints: Explain natural deduction as formal proof system
Discuss rules: Introduction and Elimination for each connective (∧, ∨, →, ¬)
Show proof examples using multiple rules
Explain assumption and discharge in conditional proof
Demonstrate step-by-step proof for a compound argument

questions.question: Prove the validity of the following argument using both truth table and natural deduction methods: 'If the program compiles (C), it runs correctly (R). If it runs correctly, it produces correct output (O). The program compiles. Therefore, it produces correct output.'
keyPoints: Identify propositions: C=compiles, R=runs correctly, O=correct output
Express argument: C→R, R→O, C ⊢ O
Truth table: Show all rows where premises T, conclusion T
Natural deduction: Show step-by-step using HS and MP
Conclusion: Argument is valid

questions.question: Discuss the concept of logical equivalence in propositional logic. Prove that P→Q is logically equivalent to ¬P∨Q and to ¬Q→¬P.
keyPoints: Define logical equivalence: P≡Q when P↔Q is tautology
Show P→Q ≡ ¬P∨Q using truth table
Show P→Q ≡ ¬Q→¬P (contrapositive)
Prove using truth tables for both
Discuss applications and importance

questions.question: Explain different types of propositions in propositional logic. Discuss tautology, contradiction, and contingent statements with examples.
keyPoints: Define atomic and compound propositions
Tautology: always true (P∨¬P, P→P)
Contradiction: always false (P∧¬P)
Contingent: depends on truth values (P∨Q)
Truth table method to determine type
Examples for each
